For SCU physics alumni Craig Benko ’10 and Bryan Berggren ’17, hands-on research, close mentorship, and long hours solving tough problems helped shape careers in the fast-moving world of photonics.

Click the link below to read their full story. ⬇️ 🤩 

https://t.co/gMLOkQNcFE

Yttrium 

Y, it’s atomic number 39.  It’s a silvery white rare earth metal that is abundant, it’s usually extracted from minerals like xenotime and bastnäsite. 

The main known uses as of (2026) Yttrium is primarily used in compounds and alloys.

✅ Electronics & Photonics Yttrium aluminum crystals doped with other elements are widely used in high-power solid-state lasers for industrial cutting/welding, medical procedures (e.g., eye surgery, dermatology), and defense applications.

✅ Superconductors Yttrium barium copper oxide is the basis for high-temperature superconductors that work at liquid nitrogen temperatures. 

These are used in power transmission cables, MRI magnets, and research toward more efficient energy systems and motors. 

✅ Phosphors & Lighting In LED lights, flat-panel displays, and fluorescent lamps, yttrium compounds provide red phosphors, which can cause respiratory Issues, Liver and Organ Effects, Reproductive Toxicity, 
testicular tissue, induce cell death, (programmed cell death) reduce testosterone, and impair semen quality.

✅ Ceramics & Alloys Enhances heat resistance, strength, and thermal shock resistance in advanced ceramics (e.g., for jet engines or nuclear reactors.

✅ Medical tracers, catalysts, and in some sensors or microwave filters.

What’s Special About Yttrium?

✅ It has unique electronic structure (partially filled 4d orbitals) that makes it excellent for creating stable compounds with optical, magnetic, and superconducting properties.

✅ Optical & Laser Performance. It produces high-power lasers with excellent beam quality.

✅ Strategic Importance

Critical for clean tech, defense, and electronics. 

✅ Biological/Research Interest 

Its ability to fine-tune material properties makes it indispensable in modern high-tech.

It’s a silvery white rare earth metal that is abundant, it’s usually extracted from minerals like xenotime and bastnäsite. The main known uses as of (2026) Yttrium is primarily used in compounds and alloys. ✅ Electronics & Photonics Yttrium aluminum crystals doped with other elements are widely used in high-power solid-state lasers for industrial cutting/welding, medical procedures (e.g., eye surgery, dermatology), and defense applications. ✅ Superconductors Yttrium barium copper oxide is the basis for high-temperature superconductors that work at liquid nitrogen temperatures. These are used in power transmission cables, MRI magnets, and research toward more efficient energy systems and motors. ✅ Phosphors & Lighting In LED lights, flat-panel displays, and fluorescent lamps, yttrium compounds provide red phosphors, which can cause respiratory Issues, Liver and Organ Effects, Reproductive Toxicity, testicular tissue, induce cell death, (programmed cell death) reduce testosterone, and impair semen quality. ✅ Ceramics & Alloys Enhances heat resistance, strength, and thermal shock resistance in advanced ceramics (e.g., for jet engines or nuclear reactors. ✅ Medical tracers, catalysts, and in some sensors or microwave filters. What’s Special About Yttrium? ✅ It has unique electronic structure (partially filled 4d orbitals) that makes it excellent for creating stable compounds with optical, magnetic, and superconducting properties. ✅ Optical & Laser Performance. It produces high-power lasers with excellent beam quality. ✅ Strategic Importance Critical for clean tech, defense, and electronics. ✅ Biological/Research Interest Its ability to fine-tune material properties makes it indispensable in modern high-tech."}}}, "rest_id": "2067697259198984693", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "389", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

Investor read:

• Funded by Defence R&D Canada IDEAS. Government money is flowing to free-space quantum hardware.
• Photonics suppliers (Coherent, Lumentum) make the mirrors and beamsplitters, but it's off-the-shelf and low margin here.
• Satellite-QKD programs benefit indirectly: lighter, cheaper receivers.

Signal to watch: whether QEYSSat-class or ESA links adopt passive receivers over adaptive optics.

text:
Market Review 20260623
- Total Carnage! What a rug pull. 
- The good news: this is not taking us by surprise. Whilst we took some paper cuts in the past 2 weeks, we've largely avoided getting caught in a big drawdown by holding a disproportionate amount of cash coming into this. 
- You may be wondering, why even have any paper cuts (small losses) at all? Because we CANNOT predict anything. We do not know WITH CERTAINTY whether the market will Up, Down or Sideways tomorrow or any time in the future. That means when we get good setups, we take the trade. But we manage our risk TIGHTLY at the same time so that if it turns out wrong, we don't lose a lot of money. But when it goes our way, we let it ride a little (eg our picks in biotech). 
- I will make a separate post about ZETA (which we bought today, in the midst of the selloff) as it will illustrate my thinking in more detail. 
- Guess what, technically the indices are still in an uptrend! But as I've said previously, the easy money is over. Even if the trend keeps going, it will be very volatile. I am a little encouraged that whilst the SPX is down 1.44% today, the equal weighted RSP is only down 0.34%. This tells me a lot of the selling has been led by mega caps. Which is no wonder. Remember our post yesterday? We talked about how the MAG7 is totally thrashed for now. 
- Similar picture, but more nasty in the Nasdaq, which is down 3.3% for the day. See the post last week on 17 Jun 2026 where I gave a high level overview of the key tech sectors like Semicon, Photonics etc.  I found it hard to get heavily involved in most of the AI Hardware related names. And the market is "funny/ironic" like this. I suspect A LOT of latecomers and retail will be rushing into the AI hardware trade / or had rushed in a few weeks ago on the dip. Why? Because they were locked out since the 30 Mar bottom. And the first chance they get to buy on a pullback 1-2 weeks ago, a lot may have rushed in. Why ironic? Because this has usually been how the market worked. Sucker in a lot of latecomers at the top. 
- How do you prevent something like this? Situational Awareness. I can't do all this in one post, and there is a lot of nuance involved. Experience and having seen many market turns in the past counts too. 
- Right now, I am still NOT prepared to call this a market top. The trend is still up, although there is a very high chance we will at least go into choppy sideways action for a while. The SPX has now come up against its 50SMA support, and we have earnings season soon. Maybe that will keep the market supported. On the bear side, the SPX higher timeframe (Weekly) MOMENTUM is threatening to cut / has nicked Down. This will imply the current lower timeframe (Daily) has turned DOWN.
- Nothing much to say about BTC and Gold, both looked headed for worse. I've warned about gold for a few months now. Can’t help anymore. 
- Enough of the pessimistic picture. WHERE do we look for opportunities? The sectors I mentioned in the 17 Jun post are still valid. Neoclouds. Cybersecurity. Biotech. Healthcare. 
- In the Neocloud space, NBIS WULF HUT etc all holding well. NBIS is still the leader. Yes some of them are also down slightly. But compared that to an AAOI, AXTI, AMD, ARM, etc and these Neoclouds will look like champs.
- In Cyber, FTNT, CRWD, PANW all looking good and when the market turns, these could potentially lead. 
- I am wary of buying individual biotech names because of the gap risk. Am expressing that view via ETFs. 
- All the best. Not advice. Risks involved.

What a rug pull. - The good news: this is not taking us by surprise. Whilst we took some paper cuts in the past 2 weeks, we've largely avoided getting caught in a big drawdown by holding a disproportionate amount of cash coming into this. - You may be wondering, why even have any paper cuts (small losses) at all? Because we CANNOT predict anything. We do not know WITH CERTAINTY whether the market will Up, Down or Sideways tomorrow or any time in the future. That means when we get good setups, we take the trade. But we manage our risk TIGHTLY at the same time so that if it turns out wrong, we don't lose a lot of money. But when it goes our way, we let it ride a little (eg our picks in biotech). - I will make a separate post about ZETA (which we bought today, in the midst of the selloff) as it will illustrate my thinking in more detail. - Guess what, technically the indices are still in an uptrend! But as I've said previously, the easy money is over. Even if the trend keeps going, it will be very volatile. I am a little encouraged that whilst the SPX is down 1.44% today, the equal weighted RSP is only down 0.34%. This tells me a lot of the selling has been led by mega caps. Which is no wonder. Remember our post yesterday? We talked about how the MAG7 is totally thrashed for now. - Similar picture, but more nasty in the Nasdaq, which is down 3.3% for the day. See the post last week on 17 Jun 2026 where I gave a high level overview of the key tech sectors like Semicon, Photonics etc. I found it hard to get heavily involved in most of the AI Hardware related names. And the market is "funny/ironic" like this. I suspect A LOT of latecomers and retail will be rushing into the AI hardware trade / or had rushed in a few weeks ago on the dip. Why? Because they were locked out since the 30 Mar bottom. And the first chance they get to buy on a pullback 1-2 weeks ago, a lot may have rushed in. Why ironic? Because this has usually been how the market worked. Sucker in a lot of latecomers at the top. - How do you prevent something like this? Situational Awareness. I can't do all this in one post, and there is a lot of nuance involved. Experience and having seen many market turns in the past counts too. - Right now, I am still NOT prepared to call this a market top. The trend is still up, although there is a very high chance we will at least go into choppy sideways action for a while. The SPX has now come up against its 50SMA support, and we have earnings season soon. Maybe that will keep the market supported. On the bear side, the SPX higher timeframe (Weekly) MOMENTUM is threatening to cut / has nicked Down. This will imply the current lower timeframe (Daily) has turned DOWN. - Nothing much to say about BTC and Gold, both looked headed for worse. I've warned about gold for a few months now. Can’t help anymore. - Enough of the pessimistic picture. WHERE do we look for opportunities? The sectors I mentioned in the 17 Jun post are still valid. Neoclouds. Cybersecurity. Biotech. Healthcare. - In the Neocloud space, NBIS WULF HUT etc all holding well. NBIS is still the leader. Yes some of them are also down slightly. But compared that to an AAOI, AXTI, AMD, ARM, etc and these Neoclouds will look like champs. - In Cyber, FTNT, CRWD, PANW all looking good and when the market turns, these could potentially lead. - I am wary of buying individual biotech names because of the gap risk. Am expressing that view via ETFs. - All the best. Not advice. Risks involved."}}}, "rest_id": "2069518316625134065", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "105", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Quantum technology is moving fast and the labs behind it need relocation partners who understand what is at stake.

Quantum computing, quantum physics, cryogenic research, photonics, sensing, and advanced materials labs are not standard moves. These environments often include dilution refrigerators, optical tables, lasers, vacuum systems, cryogenic equipment, high-value electronics, custom-built instruments, and years of active research.

At USA Lab Movers, we specialize in moving complex scientific environments with the planning, custom crating, rigging, trucking, cold-chain support, and on-site project management these projects require.

The goal is simple: protect the equipment, reduce downtime, and help researchers get back to work as quickly and safely as possible.

Read our latest article on moving quantum computing, quantum physics, and advanced research labs.

text:
totally agree on "IMO Photonics is the next supercycle but we’re closer to the beginning, a lot of the major revenue ramps begin mid 2027."

The market has already started to price in the theme, yet many of the real revenue ramps are not expected to begin until mid-2027 and beyond. That means the story is less about chasing what has already happened, and more about identifying which parts of the photonics stack will convert today’s technical bottlenecks into revenue over the next several years.

For me, the first places to look have always been the prerequisites for that ramp. Before photonics can become a true revenue supercycle, the industry needs enough manufacturing capacity and enough test infrastructure to support it. That is why I have focused heavily on two layers of the stack: fabs such as $TSEM, $GFS, $TSM, $XFAB, and $UMC, and measurement/testing names such as $FORM, $TER, $VIAV, $ATEYY, $KEYS, and $AEHR.

Of course, the broader opportunity also includes assembly, packaging, substrates, lasers, and many other layers. But in my view, fabs and testing are the two early gates. Without them, the ramp cannot scale; with them, today’s technical demand can begin turning into real, visible revenue.

text:
Here is one attempt to map the ⏳progress around co-packaged optics (CPO) from 2️⃣0️⃣2️⃣2️⃣ through expected developments in 2️⃣0️⃣2️⃣7️⃣.
Over the years, several organizations have developed CPO solutions, including Hewlett Packard Enterprise, Intel, ROCKLEY PHOTONICS LIMITED and IBM. However, Broadcom’s 25.6T CPO demo in 2022 helped bring renewed attention to the topic.
The timeline shows how major players are approaching the space differently: NVIDIA pushing toward micro ring modulator-based CPO switch availability, Broadcom advancing from early demos to 102.4T delivery, Marvell Technology linking CPO with custom XPUs, and RANOVUS Inc. targeting ASIC/XPU integration.
What is also important is the broader ecosystem effort behind CPO. System-level requirements and interoperability standards are shaping up. 
Personally, I am curious to see how this advanced technology develops beyond a few large corporations, and howa broader ecosystem can grow around CPO. 
❤️Constructive feedback is welcome ❤️

text:
🚨🚨🚨📢

$SPX $SPY $QQQ $IWM $VIX

While everyone is focused on Trump playing geopolitical ping pong with Iran, I'm watching AI and semiconductors inflate what may become the mother of all bubbles.

The real story isn't today's headlines.

It's the $700B to $750B annual AI infrastructure arms race quietly unfolding beneath the surface. 👇

1/
The hyperscalers, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, are spending like drunken sailors with unlimited credit cards.

Annual AI CapEx is projected to reach $700B to $750B.

Global AI related debt issuance could exceed $500B.

They're borrowing against the future and praying the future arrives on schedule.

2/
The biggest risk?

Stranded capital.

Today they're borrowing billions to fill data centers with power hungry Nvidia GPUs.

The problem is those assets are depreciated over 5 to 6 years.

Technology may make them obsolete in 2 to 3.

That's not innovation.

That's a future write off waiting for an earnings call.

3/
Imagine owing bondholders hundreds of billions while sitting on warehouses full of yesterday's hardware.

Silicon photonics.

Neuromorphic computing.

Custom ASICs.

If these technologies arrive faster than expected, today's AI infrastructure could become the corporate equivalent of buying a fleet of steam engines right before electricity.

4/
The spending frenzy also reveals another problem.

Free cash flow.

CapEx is consuming an extraordinary share of revenue:

• Alphabet about 46%
• Microsoft about 47%
• Meta about 54%

The more they spend, the more cash flow gets squeezed.

They're running faster just to stay in place.

5/
This is why everyone wants their own chips.

Google TPUs.

Amazon Trainium.

Meta custom silicon.

The goal isn't just performance.

The goal is survival.

They need lower operating costs to justify the debt they're piling onto their balance sheets today.

6/
Then comes the next twist.

If AI chips become dramatically more efficient, power stops being the bottleneck.

Location becomes the advantage.

Data centers may no longer need to sit next to massive power sources.

They can move closer to population centers, reducing latency and potentially turning today's expensive power agreements into tomorrow's capital allocation mistakes.

7/
And then there's the risk nobody wants to discuss.

The Inference Slump.

The entire AI spending boom assumes enterprise demand explodes.

But many AI pilot programs still struggle to generate meaningful returns.

If AI inference becomes extremely cheap, compute becomes a commodity.

And commodities don't generate bubble valuations.

8/
That's the paradox.

The hyperscalers need hyper efficient chips to save themselves.

But those same chips could eventually destroy the economics supporting today's AI infrastructure boom.

The cure may become the disease.

9/
Ultimately, Big Tech is engaged in a historic game of financial chicken.

They're borrowing hundreds of billions today.

Betting that tomorrow's technology arrives before today's debt becomes a problem.

Wall Street calls it innovation.

History often calls it a bubble.

Choose carefully which chapter we're currently writing.

If AI chips become dramatically more efficient, power stops being the bottleneck. Location becomes the advantage. Data centers may no longer need to sit next to massive power sources. They can move closer to population centers, reducing latency and potentially turning today's expensive power agreements into tomorrow's capital allocation mistakes. 7/ And then there's the risk nobody wants to discuss. The Inference Slump. The entire AI spending boom assumes enterprise demand explodes. But many AI pilot programs still struggle to generate meaningful returns. If AI inference becomes extremely cheap, compute becomes a commodity. And commodities don't generate bubble valuations. 8/ That's the paradox. The hyperscalers need hyper efficient chips to save themselves. But those same chips could eventually destroy the economics supporting today's AI infrastructure boom. The cure may become the disease. 9/ Ultimately, Big Tech is engaged in a historic game of financial chicken. They're borrowing hundreds of billions today. Betting that tomorrow's technology arrives before today's debt becomes a problem. Wall Street calls it innovation. History often calls it a bubble. Choose carefully which chapter we're currently writing."}}}, "rest_id": "2068392686759854532", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "760", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}

$COHR

COHR Tam Olarak Ne İş Yapıyor?

Coherent Corp.

Coherent, dünyanın en büyük fotonik (ışık teknolojileri) şirketlerinden biridir.

Basit anlatırsak:

NVDA hesap yapıyor.✅

 MU veriyi depoluyor.✅

 COHR ise bu verinin veri merkezinde ışıkla taşınmasını sağlıyor. ✅

Yani yapay zekanın sinir sistemi diyebileceğimiz katmanda yer alıyor. (İşte en can alıcı nokta bu )

Şirketin Ana İş Kolları : 

1. Optik Transceiver'lar

Bunlar veri merkezlerinde kullanılan özel modüller.

Görevleri:

GPU → GPU
Sunucu → Sunucu
Rack → Rack

arasındaki veriyi ışıkla taşımak.

AI modelleri büyüdükçe bu ürünlerin önemi katlanıyor. ⚡️⚡️

2. Silicon Photonics

Bence şirketin en kritik alanı.

Elektrik sinyallerini ışığa dönüştürüyor.

Avantajları:

Daha az enerji tüketimi✅

Daha yüksek hız✅

Daha düşük gecikme✅

Daha az ısı✅

NVIDIA'nın gelecek nesil veri merkezi mimarisinde tam olarak ihtiyaç duyulan teknoloji bu.

3. Lazerler

COHR dünyanın en büyük endüstriyel ve telekom lazer üreticilerinden biri.

Ürettikleri:

InP (Indium Phosphide) lazerler
CW lazerler
VCSEL lazerler
Telekom lazerleri

Bu lazerler hem kendi ürünlerinde hem de sektörün diğer oyuncularında kullanılıyor.

4. Co-Packaged Optics (CPO)

Şu anda Wall Street'in en heyecanlandığı konu.

Bugün:

GPU ↔ Optik modül ayrı.

Yarın:

GPU ↔ Optik modül aynı pakette olacak.

Bu teknolojiye CPO deniyor.

NVIDIA'nın gelecekteki veri merkezi planlarının merkezinde bu teknoloji var. 

COHR da burada ana tedarikçilerden biri olmaya çalışıyor.

Kimlerle Çalışıyor?

Kesin müşteri listeleri her zaman açıklanmıyor ancak sektör analizlerinde ve şirket sunumlarında şu isimler sürekli karşımıza çıkıyor:

NVIDIA
Microsoft Azure
Amazon Web Services
Meta
Google Cloud

Çünkü bunların hepsi devasa AI veri merkezleri kuruyor ve optik ağ altyapısına ihtiyaç duyuyorlar.

NVIDIA ile İlişkisi

Burası çok önemli.

2026'da $NVDA :

Coherent'e yaklaşık 2 milyar dolar yatırım yaptı.

Uzun vadeli satın alma anlaşmaları yaptı.

Lazer ve optik bileşen kapasitesini garanti altına aldı.

Bu haber çıktığında COHR hissesi sert yükselmişti.

Çünkü bu durum:

"NVIDIA gelecekte fotoniğin kritik olacağını düşünüyor."

mesajını verdi.

Neden Yapay Zeka İçin Kritik?

Bugün darboğaz:

GPU sayısı değil.

GPU'ların birbirleriyle haberleşmesi.

Rakipleri Kimler?

En büyük rakipler:

Lumentum Holdings $LITE 

Applied Optoelectronics $AAOI 

Corning $GLW 

Broadcom $AVGO 

Marvell Technology $MRVL

Ama COHR'nin farkı:

Lazer + Transceiver + Silicon Photonics + CPO bileşenlerini aynı çatı altında sunabilmesi.

text:
This week I learned the photonics trade is finally shifting from vibes to contracts.

$AXTI got a RMB173M / USD25.4M InP wafer deal with prepay/minimum-buy language. IQE got Tower commitments. AEHR got a follow-on SiPh test order.

Still tiny-scout territory. Proof is not size.
Go $GLW guys if you want to participate in the Photonics scale-up 😉

text:
The 5 best stocks and ETFs to own right now: 👇

$DRAM - memory ETF that includes $SNDK, $MU, Samsung and more. This is a growing space and will only continue to grow.

$TSLA - high and volatile gas prices are increasing demand for EVs. Energy storage systems, charging stations and autonomous capabilities are growing immensely. Robots are coming soon.

$SOFI - with oil stabilizing over time, the chances for rate cuts due to lower inflation is higher. This company is highly profitable and is scaling up quickly. They’re projecting 40% growth over the next 3-4 years.

$AAOI - photonics is a huge market and is growing in demand as AI data centers scale. These guys are positioned well to crush it over the next 5+ years. 

$ONDS - there has been a huge increase in the demand of autonomous drones, boats and on land vehicles used in military operations across the globe. Ondas has all the right products and services the U.S. military and other allied nations would need. 

What 5 stocks would you choose? 🤔

Yet its revenue model remains heavily linked to finished transceiver units. When thinking about their insulation to oversupply... AAOI offers hyperscalers: > A U.S.-anchored supply chain > Internal laser technology > Automated manufacturing > Large planned 800G and 1.6T capacity > TAA-compatible product options > A hedge against China concentration > CPO and ELSFP optionality They say that U.S. production is attractive to customers and that automation limits the cost penalty associated with domestic manufacturing. Demand currently exceeds planned capacity through mid-2027. Even with the massive capacity ramp. text:
Why i bought $aeva at $14 in May 2026 and Why i will buy it again at $24

The legacy LiDAR market is heavily saturated with companies building systems that are essentially just rapid 3D cameras. Aeva Technologies ($AEVA) is completely rewriting this architectural standard. By transitioning from traditional time-of-flight sensors to Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) technology, Aeva is moving machine perception from static imagery to real-time 4D understanding.
Here is exactly why $AEVA is positioned as a foundational deep-tech asset as we approach the next wave of autonomous infrastructure:
  Traditional LiDAR simply measures the time it takes for a laser pulse to return, providing basic distance data. Aeva’s FMCW continuous modulation captures not just depth and reflectivity, but per-point instant velocity at the pixel level. This means the sensor instantly knows exactly how fast an object is moving in real-time. This precision is non-negotiable for Level 3 and Level 4 autonomous driving, where high-speed decision-making is required.
  The most significant structural validation for Aeva recently occurred when its 4D LiDAR was selected for the NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion autonomous vehicle reference platform. Aeva and NVIDIA will collaborate to integrate this perception platform into Hyperion to support production vehicle programs targeted in 2028. Furthermore, Aeva has already secured an exclusive LiDAR supplier role (outside China) for a top European passenger vehicle OEM, with production also targeted for 2028.
  Aeva is rapidly expanding beyond civilian applications and directly into the highly lucrative defense sector. In Q1 2026, the CEO highlighted that Aeva’s technology is being integrated into a second autonomous ground vehicle through an expansion with Forterra. A massive tactical advantage of Aeva's specific hardware is its wavelength undetectability by night vision systems, opening up immediate expansion opportunities for military drones and ground operations.
  While many hardware startups struggle with cash burn, Aeva is showing definitive commercial momentum. In Q1 2026, the company reported a record revenue of $6.3 million, marking a 90% year-over-year increase. While the company still operates at a net loss of nearly $35 million, this revenue surge indicates a successful scaling transition from laboratory testing to securing major customer development milestones.
Aeva is no longer just pitching a whitepaper; their silicon-photonics hardware is actively being baked into the global automotive architecture of the future.

Aeva Technologies ($AEVA) is completely rewriting this architectural standard. By transitioning from traditional time-of-flight sensors to Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) technology, Aeva is moving machine perception from static imagery to real-time 4D understanding. Here is exactly why $AEVA is positioned as a foundational deep-tech asset as we approach the next wave of autonomous infrastructure: Traditional LiDAR simply measures the time it takes for a laser pulse to return, providing basic distance data. Aeva’s FMCW continuous modulation captures not just depth and reflectivity, but per-point instant velocity at the pixel level. This means the sensor instantly knows exactly how fast an object is moving in real-time. This precision is non-negotiable for Level 3 and Level 4 autonomous driving, where high-speed decision-making is required. The most significant structural validation for Aeva recently occurred when its 4D LiDAR was selected for the NVIDIA DRIVE Hyperion autonomous vehicle reference platform. Aeva and NVIDIA will collaborate to integrate this perception platform into Hyperion to support production vehicle programs targeted in 2028. Furthermore, Aeva has already secured an exclusive LiDAR supplier role (outside China) for a top European passenger vehicle OEM, with production also targeted for 2028. Aeva is rapidly expanding beyond civilian applications and directly into the highly lucrative defense sector. In Q1 2026, the CEO highlighted that Aeva’s technology is being integrated into a second autonomous ground vehicle through an expansion with Forterra. A massive tactical advantage of Aeva's specific hardware is its wavelength undetectability by night vision systems, opening up immediate expansion opportunities for military drones and ground operations. While many hardware startups struggle with cash burn, Aeva is showing definitive commercial momentum. In Q1 2026, the company reported a record revenue of $6.3 million, marking a 90% year-over-year increase. While the company still operates at a net loss of nearly $35 million, this revenue surge indicates a successful scaling transition from laboratory testing to securing major customer development milestones. Aeva is no longer just pitching a whitepaper; their silicon-photonics hardware is actively being baked into the global automotive architecture of the future. $AEVA #LiDAR #AutonomousVehicles #DeepTech #FMCW #NvidiaDrive"}}}, "rest_id": "2068770017777524798", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "656", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Market's reaction to $NOK is crazy

This is what happened recently: 
- $NVDA purchasing stake 
- $4B US manufacturing push. 
- AI & Cloud customer sales +49% in Q1
- Insiders purchased stock at higher than current price

And still stock fell this week after the 2x YTD run into mid-June. Nokia is now a credible photonics & AI-networking supplier that demands re-rating. 

👀I'm watching one thing: Growth in AI & cloud customers. The whole thesis lives there.

Bought the NVIDIA-Nokia headline and feel unhappy? Personal Opinion : Hold and wait, better days are waiting .  

What's your cost basis ?

NFA. DYOR. Hold long position

text:
I'm warning you now.

If hyperscalers do double current issuance expectations, these are the stocks to buy:

Highest alignment are core data-center/ interconnect/memory/capacity plays (listed below).

$SIVE, $IQE, $AAOI, $OPTX: Photonics/optics enablers (lasers, epi-wafers, transceivers, custom optics). Critical for scale-out networking and CPO in larger AI clusters. Higher hyperscaler spend = more fiber/interconnect demand. 

$MU: HBM memory. Hyperscalers are the primary buyers; supply is already sold out for 2026 with multi-year agreements. Accelerated capex reinforces the supercycle.

$IREN, $BRUN, $CRWV, $HIVE, $CLSK: AI cloud/HPC/GPU capacity and power infrastructure providers. Hyperscalers buy capacity or compete in the buildout; more spend supports power and compute demand.

$ASYS, $ICHR, $INTT: Semiconductor equipment/subsystems/test for AI GPU packaging and chip production ramp. Direct beneficiary of higher chip volumes needed for hyperscaler clusters.

$PENG: Advanced computing + memory solutions for AI/HPC deployments.

Moderate/edge alignment:

$AMPG: RF/microwave amplifiers and 5G/O-RAN subsystems. Benefits from broader AI infrastructure (AI-RAN, edge wireless, satellite for distributed setups) and new customers like NVIDIA/Amazon. Less direct than pure DC enablers but positive from overall spend.

$SILC, $NOK: Networking/telecom infrastructure supporting AI data flows and edge.

$XFAB: Specialty foundry for power/analog/sensors used in AI systems.

Lower or indirect:

$MCHP, $MX, $AMBQ: More diversified/edge-focused; some benefit but less concentrated exposure.

$AKAM: Edge delivery; indirect tailwind from overall AI growth.

I'm really sticking to the main high alignment plays (that I've been saying for a bit).

$OPTX, $ASYS, $IQE, $HIVE $BRUN in particular are the plays I like.

Buy any of the good ones listed above, and I believe you will perform great over 2026-2027.

Just my 2c on what happens next.

Any other good plays that I'm missing here?

text:
While everyone is focused on Trump playing geopolitical ping pong with Iran, I'm watching AI and semiconductors inflate what may become the mother of all bubbles.

The real story isn't today's headlines.

It's the $700B to $750B annual AI infrastructure arms race quietly unfolding beneath the surface. 👇

1/
The hyperscalers, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, are spending like drunken sailors with unlimited credit cards.

Annual AI CapEx is projected to reach $700B to $750B.

Global AI related debt issuance could exceed $500B.

They're borrowing against the future and praying the future arrives on schedule.

2/
The biggest risk?

Stranded capital.

Today they're borrowing billions to fill data centers with power hungry Nvidia GPUs.

The problem is those assets are depreciated over 5 to 6 years.

Technology may make them obsolete in 2 to 3.

That's not innovation.

That's a future write off waiting for an earnings call.

3/
Imagine owing bondholders hundreds of billions while sitting on warehouses full of yesterday's hardware.

Silicon photonics.

Neuromorphic computing.

Custom ASICs.

If these technologies arrive faster than expected, today's AI infrastructure could become the corporate equivalent of buying a fleet of steam engines right before electricity.

4/
The spending frenzy also reveals another problem.

Free cash flow.

CapEx is consuming an extraordinary share of revenue:

• Alphabet about 46%
• Microsoft about 47%
• Meta about 54%

The more they spend, the more cash flow gets squeezed.

They're running faster just to stay in place.

5/
This is why everyone wants their own chips.

Google TPUs.

Amazon Trainium.

Meta custom silicon.

The goal isn't just performance.

The goal is survival.

They need lower operating costs to justify the debt they're piling onto their balance sheets today.

6/
Then comes the next twist.

If AI chips become dramatically more efficient, power stops being the bottleneck.

Location becomes the advantage.

Data centers may no longer need to sit next to massive power sources.

They can move closer to population centers, reducing latency and potentially turning today's expensive power agreements into tomorrow's capital allocation mistakes.

7/
And then there's the risk nobody wants to discuss.

The Inference Slump.

The entire AI spending boom assumes enterprise demand explodes.

But many AI pilot programs still struggle to generate meaningful returns.

If AI inference becomes extremely cheap, compute becomes a commodity.

And commodities don't generate bubble valuations.

8/
That's the paradox.

The hyperscalers need hyper efficient chips to save themselves.

But those same chips could eventually destroy the economics supporting today's AI infrastructure boom.

The cure may become the disease.

9/
Ultimately, Big Tech is engaged in a historic game of financial chicken.

They're borrowing hundreds of billions today.

Betting that tomorrow's technology arrives before today's debt becomes a problem.

Wall Street calls it innovation.

History often calls it a bubble.

Choose carefully which chapter we're currently writing.

The real story isn't today's headlines. It's the $700B to $750B annual AI infrastructure arms race quietly unfolding beneath the surface. 👇 1/ The hyperscalers, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, are spending like drunken sailors with unlimited credit cards. Annual AI CapEx is projected to reach $700B to $750B. Global AI related debt issuance could exceed $500B. They're borrowing against the future and praying the future arrives on schedule. 2/ The biggest risk? Stranded capital. Today they're borrowing billions to fill data centers with power hungry Nvidia GPUs. The problem is those assets are depreciated over 5 to 6 years. Technology may make them obsolete in 2 to 3. That's not innovation. That's a future write off waiting for an earnings call. 3/ Imagine owing bondholders hundreds of billions while sitting on warehouses full of yesterday's hardware. Silicon photonics. Neuromorphic computing. Custom ASICs. If these technologies arrive faster than expected, today's AI infrastructure could become the corporate equivalent of buying a fleet of steam engines right before electricity. 4/ The spending frenzy also reveals another problem. Free cash flow. CapEx is consuming an extraordinary share of revenue: • Alphabet about 46% • Microsoft about 47% • Meta about 54% The more they spend, the more cash flow gets squeezed. They're running faster just to stay in place. 5/ This is why everyone wants their own chips. Google TPUs. Amazon Trainium. Meta custom silicon. The goal isn't just performance. The goal is survival. They need lower operating costs to justify the debt they're piling onto their balance sheets today. 6/ Then comes the next twist. If AI chips become dramatically more efficient, power stops being the bottleneck. Location becomes the advantage. Data centers may no longer need to sit next to massive power sources. They can move closer to population centers, reducing latency and potentially turning today's expensive power agreements into tomorrow's capital allocation mistakes. 7/ And then there's the risk nobody wants to discuss. The Inference Slump. The entire AI spending boom assumes enterprise demand explodes. But many AI pilot programs still struggle to generate meaningful returns. If AI inference becomes extremely cheap, compute becomes a commodity. And commodities don't generate bubble valuations. 8/ That's the paradox. The hyperscalers need hyper efficient chips to save themselves. But those same chips could eventually destroy the economics supporting today's AI infrastructure boom. The cure may become the disease. 9/ Ultimately, Big Tech is engaged in a historic game of financial chicken. They're borrowing hundreds of billions today. Betting that tomorrow's technology arrives before today's debt becomes a problem. Wall Street calls it innovation. History often calls it a bubble. Choose carefully which chapter we're currently writing."}}}, "rest_id": "2068750420823163302", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "190", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$QQQ 
At ~$660, within a 52-week range of $402 to $688. The Nasdaq was the week’s clear standout, jumping 2.4% as the chip complex caught fire on a landmark deal. President Trump announced that Intel would design and build chips domestically alongside Apple, sending Intel surging 11.45% Thursday and dragging the entire semiconductor group higher. It was a broad AI and chip melt-up: SanDisk climbed 11.63%, Super Micro added 10.10%, and Enphase jumped 10% on production shipments of its IQ9S microinverters plus a Barclays upgrade. Data-center and photonics names followed, with Corning up 7%, Credo up 5%, and Coherent up 4%. The rally is notable because it came in the same week the Fed turned hawkish and Treasury yields spiked, conditions that usually punish long-duration tech. Instead, the Intel-Apple news and the peace deal overwhelmed the rate fear. SpaceX, fresh off last week’s record $1.77 trillion IPO, anchors Saylor’s new “Magnificent 8” framing. The one persistent worry remains concentration: a handful of AI-linked megacaps still account for nearly 40% of the entire index, so the Nasdaq’s fate is tied to whether this chip enthusiasm holds through Micron’s earnings next week.

The Nasdaq shrugged off a hawkish Fed and surging yields to rip 2.4%, led by Intel up 11.45% on the Apple chip deal. When tech rallies through a yield spike, the AI narrative is doing the heavy lifting. Micron earnings next week are the test of whether it is justified.

text:
$TSM $UMC $GFS $ASX $AMKR $INTC

TSMC's extremely bullish projections at Taiwan Technology Symposium from last Month. Must Read.

AI Wafer Demand: Projected to rise 11x from 2022 to 2026 for AI accelerator wafers. 

Capacity Expansion: Advanced process nodes (2nm and A16) → 70% CAGR from 2026–2028.

CoWoS advanced packaging → >80% CAGR from 2022–2027.

Semiconductor Market Forecast: TSMC now expects the global chip market to exceed $1.5 trillion by 2030 (up from a prior $1 trillion estimate), fueled by AI. 

Regional Demand: Asia-Pacific customers consumed over 2.1 million 12-inch equivalent wafers last year, equivalent to stacking more than three Taipei 101 towers. 

Plans for nine new phases of wafer fabs and advanced packaging facilities in 2026. 

Arizona ramp: First fab in production, second in tool move-in (2H26), third under construction; fourth fab + packaging site starting this year. Arizona output expected to grow 1.8x YoY by 2026 with Taiwan-comparable yields. 

Technology Roadmap: CoWoS: Current 5.5-reticle version (world’s largest) at 98% yield. Plans include a 14-reticle version with 20 HBM stacks in 2028, scaling to >14 reticles and up to 24 HBM stacks in 2029. 

SoW (System on Wafer): Can integrate up to 64 HBM stacks + 16 CoWoS modules (>40 reticles). Logic-only version in production (2024); HBM-integrated version targeted for 2029. 

SoIC (3D stacking): Massive improvements in interconnect density and efficiency; scaling to finer pitches (down to 4.5-micron by A14). 

COUPE (Silicon Photonics): 200Gbps Micro Ring Modulator now in production, 4x better energy efficiency and 10x lower latency than copper

text:
I called $IQE at 17p.

Not everyone believed it. The thesis was compound semiconductor epitaxy as a structural chokepoint in AI infrastructure backed by trough revenues, a near monopoly position in InP epiwafers, and a valuation that made no sense relative to peers.

It's now at 49p. That's nearly a 3x from entry. And I'm just getting started.

What makes me most happy right now isn't the price. It's watching this community grow around genuine, research backed conviction, investors who took the time to understand the supply chain, the technology, and why this name matters far beyond what consensus was pricing in.

The fundamentals today are stronger than when I entered:

In the last 60 days alone a multi-year supply agreement with MACOM ($MTSI) in April, and a multi-year InP epiwafer deal with Tower Semiconductor ($TSEM) in June, directly tied to Tower's $1.3B in AI silicon photonics contracts. Two tier-1 customers. Two long-term contracts. Revenue visibility where there was none.

The thesis reminder:

IQE is the only Western pure play listed epi wafer manufacturer. It trades at 6.9x P/S on trough revenues down 42% from its 2022 peak. 
Its closest listed pure-play peer, Landmark, trades at 76x P/S with 48.6% gross margins.
The entire valuation gap between these two companies exists for one reason: IQE hasn't yet proven it can convert a favorable cycle into margin. 
That proof is now coming through contracted revenue, rising epi wafer prices, and an AI optical infrastructure buildout that has barely started.

The target:
A 3x from here c.152p implies IQE trading at roughly 20x P/S. 

That is still a 73% discount to Landmark. It is not an aggressive number. 
It is the bare minimum re-rating that comes when the market acknowledges IQE's position in the AI photonics supply chain is structural, not cyclical.
The longer term case, if revenues recover toward peak and margins normalize, is far larger.

Thank you to everyone who has followed this thesis since 17p. The best part of this community is the quality of the conversation. Keep it coming. 💪

Not financial advice. Do your own due diligence

Every time you use ChatGPT, Gemini or Claude, thousands of GPUs are working together simultaneously. Most people focus on the GPU. Nobody talks about the real bottleneck. 2/6. GPUs don't think alone. Training GPT-4 required 25,000 GPUs running in parallel, synchronizing billions of times per second. The problem: copper cables can't move that much data, that fast, over any meaningful distance. They overheat. They consume too much power. They hit a wall. 3/6.The solution is photonics: converting electrons into photons (light) to move data at the speed of light through optical fiber. But light doesn't come from silicon. It comes from a compound called Indium Phosphide (InP) — a crystal that naturally emits light at exactly the right wavelength for fiber optics. 4/6.Here's where the value chain gets interesting. Before any laser can be made, someone has to grow InP crystals, slice them into wafers, then deposit ultra-thin atomic layers on them (a process called epitaxy). That last step, epitaxy, is the most technically complex in the entire chain. It's where functional properties are created at the nanometer level. 5/6.The global leader in that epitaxy step is $IQE, listed in London. They sit at the base of the entire AI photonics supply chain. Their wafers end up in: every optical transceiver connecting GPUs in AI datacenters, Apple's Face ID lasers, US military radar systems, next-gen satellite constellations. 6/6. Microsoft just committed $500B to AI infrastructure. Meta, Google, AWS are all scaling fast. Every dollar spent on AI datacenters increases demand for InP wafers and IQE makes them. Market cap: £150m. Strategic review underway. Lazard mandated. Q1 2026 order book: strong. EV/Sales: 1.9x vs 3.5-4.5x for US peers. Not investment advice. text:
Applied Energetics (OTCQB: AERG) CEO Chris Donaghey joined Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit panel on the future of battlefield autonomy in New York.

Applied Energetics, Inc. (@AELaserTech)  (OTCQB: AERG), a developer of ultrashort pulse laser and directed energy technologies, took part in Morgan Stanley’s inaugural National Security Innovation Summit in New York on June 15, 2026, where President and CEO Chris Donaghey joined the panel “Machines in Motion: The Future of Battlefield Autonomy.”

Applied Energetics is a B2i Digital (@b2idigital) Featured Company. See the company’s profile at https://t.co/D4G4jB95ZV

The summit convened defense companies and institutional investors for a day of panels on national security technology, from autonomy and AI to space and missiles. On the battlefield-autonomy panel, Donaghey appeared alongside executives from Forterra, Firestorm Labs, and Lockheed Martin, in a discussion moderated by Morgan Stanley’s Kristine Liwag.

The themes ran close to Applied Energetics’ core work. According to the company, its ultrashort pulse laser portfolio targets counter-UAS, counter-sensor, infrared countermeasure, and other emerging directed energy missions, and aligns with the U.S. Department of War’s Critical Technology Area of Scaled Directed Energy, an area in sharper focus as militaries weigh how to counter low-cost drones at scale.

Visit the company’s website to learn more: https://t.co/Pd6aX43XBx

About Applied Energetics: Applied Energetics, Inc. specializes in advanced laser and photonics systems, particularly fiber-based ultrashort pulse (USP) laser technologies. With 26 patents and 2 patents pending, the company’s proprietary architecture is designed to enable size, weight, and power reductions relative to traditional continuous wave laser technology. AE’s dual-use systems target integration on defense platforms for high-intensity USP delivery, with applications across national security and commercial markets.

For investor information, contact Kevin McGrath, Managing Director, Cameron Associates, Inc., at kevin@cameronassoc.com or 646-418-7002. For additional information, visit https://t.co/VWa2WnbInK and https://t.co/4uzid7hTcd

Disclosure: David Shapiro, Chief Executive Officer of B2i Digital, personally purchased in the open market and currently owns shares of unrestricted AERG stock, in line with B2i Digital’s practice of investing alongside its Featured Companies. See the complete Disclosure at https://t.co/ljTVqQL4Si. B2i Digital is not a broker-dealer or investment adviser. This post is informational and not an offer or solicitation regarding any security.

text:
I want to address a common misconception  or maybe just ignorance I see on the part of numerous FinX influencers who clearly don’t understand the hedge fund business.  I don’t say that to be disparaging, but bc many of the comparisons I see are misleading and totally illogical really.   So if you see anyone do the following, take whatever they say with a  large grain of salt!

What I have seen are many individuals with what I would describe as unusually high returns this year going on and on about how they are outperforming this hedge fund and that hedge fund and how stupid people are to be paying large fees to those funds.  Now to be fair there are some crappy funds out there that don’t deserve the fees that they get, and that’s on the institutions that allocate large sums of capital without doing the proper due diligence.

But to compare one’s returns when one is running a 10 stock levered long portfolio of the usual AI/Optics/Space along with some https://t.co/Sy3Fyeq798 and $LPTH for defense tech exposure with those of Millennium, Citadel, Point 72, Tudor, etc is absurd.  Why?  Because the individuals going all in and margining w/ this stuff are only focused on return, they do not consider risk.  Why consider risk when everything is up and to the right?  

The top tier hedge funds are obsessed with risk management  and capital preservation.  They seek to avoid Cathie Wood style permanent capital destruction type investments at all costs.  You can laugh bc they are up 20% this year while you’re up 200%,  but that would be pretty foolish.  The “platform” hedge funds businsesses tend to run almost fully hedged long/short.  They don’t seek to ride beta, they seek to grind out 5-7% unlevered returns with little risk and then lever them 300-400%.  That is how they deliver all-weather performance over an entire business cycle.  Through the ups and the downs.  So when you are inevitably down 50%, they may be up 5-10% that year.  Beta reverts to the mean in time, alpha doesnt. 

The last time I saw these nonsensical comparisons was in 1999 when housewives became day-traders and often made fun of their hedge funds husbands who they were outperforming with stellar picks like VerticalNet, Nortel, Lucent, CMGI, and yes…even CIEN for DWDM.  This is CIEN’s second go around as a hot stock, most don’t know it was similarly en fuego in the late 90s just for different applications in fiber/telecom, not yet in photonics. 

That’s all.  Just wanted to make sure that when u see folks post crazy returns for  their concentrated high-risk and often margined portfolios, you realize that comparing them with the returns of the tried and true HF platforms makes little sense.  They have withstood the test of time, the influencer bragging about their returns has not.

Have a great long weekend everyone.  

JG

text:
Entries in the Global Photonics Engineering Contest 2026 will be judged on:

- Technical innovation and system-level thinking

- How effectively the solution addresses a real engineering challenge

- Clear and credible use of Photonic Chip technology

- Technical and commercial feasibility

- Quality of diagrams, schematics, and supporting visuals

The contest entries will be reviewed by a multidisciplinary jury panel of experts from PhotonDelta, ALUVIA Photonics, Bright Photonics, Epiphany-Design, imec, Ligentec, LioniX International, PHIX Photonics Assembly, SMART Photonics, XIVER MEMS Foundry, New Origin, PhotonVentures, and SBT Industries.

Learn more and submit your applications here: https://t.co/RxU4cUDzZe

#photonics #intergatedphotonics #PICs #engineering #technology

text:
Aehr secures follow-on order for AI data center optical interconnect

Aehr Test Systems received a follow-on production order for a fully automated FOX-XP wafer-level burn-in (WLBI) system. This system, configured to test nine wafers in parallel, includes a WaferPak Auto Aligner and FOX WaferPak Contactors, with delivery expected within six months. The customer, a global leader in networking products, plans to use the system for advanced silicon photonics-based transceivers for hyperscale AI and cloud data centers. (https://t.co/8OBYA2YSGp)

Vik: Any guesses who this "customer" is? This is a "follow on" order mind you -- the first one was in April for $41M (https://t.co/H5J7MpXS5J). I'll just say Broadcom. If you disagree, leave a comment below.

@austinsemis @vikramskr

text:
Entegris $ENTG has quickly gone up 38% in just 7 days. 

It is now at 100x PE. I have not considered taking profit but maybe I will take some.

That being said, there is some recent good news for the business. 

> Entegris did a cross licensing deal with JSR and Inpria that ends a patent dispute they had over EUV photoresist and filtration technologies. Now, the companies will work together on the same matter. 

In my personal opinion, if you want exposure to the AI trade, then the lowest risk will come in the chemicals/materials and equipment side.

Many photonics names have quickly become overvalued. As long as the WFE market remains strong, the market of chemicals/materials will keep growing.

Here is my thesis: 

This is a well established business founded in 1966. 

Their products and solutions include: filtration products for gases & fluids, liquid systems, gas delivery systems, specialty chemistries for deposition and cleaning, wafer carriers and shippers, specialty coatings, premium graphite and silicon carbide, and shippers and trays for protecting and transporting disk drive components, and CMP slurries.

Basically a one stop shop for semiconductor companies, with ~94% of revenue related to semiconductor.

They report two segments: Materials Solutions (MS) and Advanced Purity Solutions (APS), but in the financial report for their 10-Q they break it down by:

- Semiconductor (fabs, equipment and engineering, chemical and materials, semi distributor and other)

- Non-semi

Think of MS as materials only and APS as filtration products.

The company controls roughly 35% to 40% of the global market for microcontamination control (liquid and gas filtration) and holds top-tier share positions in CMP (Chemical Mechanical Planarization) slurries, pads, and specialized wafer carriers. 

Additionally, Entegris and Pall Corporation (a subsidiary of Danaher) are the dominant duopoly leaders, controlling over 60% of the electronic filtration and purification market combined. Entegris specifically holds about 28% of the semiconductor liquid filter market. 

Why does this matter? 

As microchips continue to shrink, transitioning from 14nm nodes to 1.4nm, the margin for error shrinks alongside them. In fact, the allowable defect tolerance has plummeted tenfold, from 7nm to a mere 0.7nm. At this microscopic scale, even the slightest solid, liquid, or gaseous contamination can result in catastrophic financial damage. Entegris estimates that a mere 1% drop in production yield at the 1.4nm stage can drain up to $800 million annually from a fabrication facility. 

The company works hand-in-hand with its clients to design its purification systems directly into the end-to-end manufacturing systems. Because advanced chipmaking is so incredibly detailed, swapping out an established Entegris system for a rival product on an active production line is practically unfeasible which makes Entegris a long term success as long as chips keep getting more advanced. 

Per Q1'26: 

MS sales grew 2.8% YoY while APS sales grew 6.8% YoY. 

MS sales are ~43% of revenue; APS is ~57% 

They did suffer from sales decrease QoQ but that is mostly seasonality based (not a big deal).

Growth geographically is mixed for the most part, while sales of MS and APS are declining in North America, sales of APS significantly down in Japan...they are compensating this by growth in Taiwan for APS and MS.

The company's gross margin and operating margin have both increased YoY. 

The company is expected to finish FY26 at $3.63 EPS, a 134% increase YoY due to stronger AI demand. For FY27, it is estimated to be $4.65, dropping P/E from ~70x to 28x. 

Additional info:

They are also a confirmed supplier to SK Hynix for molybdenum NAND deposition. They supply Molybdenum Dichloride Dioxide (MoO₂Cl₂) which has seen adoption increase because it is cost effective, reduces resistivity in narrow features, and has an advantage over copper for interconnections by not needing a barrier layer.

Tungsten starts failing as NAND starts stacking above 300 layers, which is why molybdenum is seeing adoption.

Customers include:

Micron $MU
Intel $INTC
SK Hynix $000660.KS
Samsung $005930.KS
TSMC $TSM 
ASML $ASML 
Lam Research $LRCX
Tokyo Electron $803.5T
Applied Materials $AMAT

You can argue it is expensive right now, but there is nobody else that can benefit in this space like them and it is not a foreign company."}}}, "previous_counts": {"bookmark_count": 0, "favorite_count": 0, "quote_count": 0, "reply_count": 0, "retweet_count": 0}, "rest_id": "2065108957933310219", "source": "<a href="https://mobile.twitter.com" rel="nofollow">Twitter Web App</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "11899", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2067683962353225827", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/android" rel="nofollow">Twitter for Android</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3563", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Reality check:

• Fabrication paper, not a quantum result. No single-photon purity, no spin coherence, no entanglement.
• 82.9% is optical-coupling yield, not qubit yield.
• Coupling efficiency stays qualitative ("guided emission observed"), no dB number.
• Room temp, 6 nm lines, far from operating conditions.

But zoom out:

Manufacturability is exactly the unglamorous wall that has kept color-center quantum stuck at hero-device demos. Wafer-scale lithography at 82.9% yield is a real step out of that trap.

Direction: the diamond-photonics scaling story is shifting from "can we make one" to "can we make hundreds."

• Caltech LEO comb, Cisco quantum switch, Harvard/NIST Yb-in-alumina

No pick-and-place. The boring bottleneck of diamond photonics just got less boring. 🧵 https://t.co/J3JE5zn8mG

text:
"AI data centers hit a new bottleneck, optical interconnects"

When their hypes are over, now their reality in AI chip supply chain. 

I don't need to be charted, if I was selected at my start.

Your investors should know : Manufacturing in testing layers means where optical interconnect scaling and silicon photonics capacity are emerging as key constraints on AI data centre buildout. 

Which aligns with this week's observation, where AI data centres handles the scaling in larger workloads, in sell-offs, would they trigger the physical infrastructure connecting chips inside those facilities has becoming a limiting factor. 

Don't stir round their bs, this is fact. 

"Optical interconnects, which use light rather than electrical signals to move data, increased to be seen as the solution to bandwidth and power constraints at scale."

You tell me how this shift towards? 
It's not a design challenge, but more of "manufacturing and testing challenge", such as equipment suppliers, test solution providers, contract manufacturers, all play roles in determining how quickly optical interconnects technology can react high-volume production.

You tested that before, I know, how's the sudden pull-off, as if capacity running out, did you find remedies, when the heat is up, more volumes to scale increases of demands creation for specialised equipment and test infrastructure across several companies, typically component availability could associate as near-term execution risks. New partnerships and equipment order announcements signal that the photonics manufacturing ecosystem is highly depend on, actively expanding t meet AI data centre demand. 

Revenue targeting, your thought on these, AI chip supply chain in focus : semiconductor equipment, process control, foundry services, specialty chip design, all these companies in your industry.

Metrics: 
- CapEx, < 80%, over 100%, high risk 
- Free cash flow, keep slipping, nope. 
- Cloud services revenue and profitability, matched? 
- Management's perception : continual and rising demands, now changed into the tone of "Capital efficiency" to "slow the tempo down". 

A whole rinse up, bubbles to all their fluffs. 
The rest that can be existed is actually running with the infrastructure, users centric, enterprise architecture, may I remind you? 

If their pimps are still sitting at RFPs levels, they can't let you know how I wrote here, what makes you think I looked at those since 2019? :D

That's why 2024 dropped, 2025, unbothered and pushed harder, now what, you want to shut who up?

For sure, shut yours up next if they don't know what "AI adoption" means! 

What compliance project are you sourcing out? 
What's your market like now? :D

text:
💡Montana is well-equipped with a supply chain for a 21st century economy💡

In collaboration with the @MontanaChamber, our Policy Director @TannerMAvery recently gave a presentation on what data centers mean for Montana.🖥️ Highlighting that Montana has abundant critical minerals, high-tech photonics, and chip-manufacturing, Tanner shares: “when I think about data centers, I don’t just think that we should attract data centers for the sake of data centers. I’m thinking that when we can attract parts of the supply chain, we can attract the entire supply chain.”

Check out this clip! 👇 Link to full video below🔗

text:
$XFAB upgraded to Outperform with a €12.8 PT by Bernstein — up from €5.

Bullish call. But I think it's still undershooting the real optionality here.

🧵

The obvious thesis:

Automotive recovery + SiC/power semi ramp. Classic reactive upgrade. Bernstein is pricing in the cycle, not the platform.

What the market isn't pricing in yet:

$XFAB is quietly building a silicon photonics foundry capability through PhotonixFAB — in partnership with NVIDIA and LIGENTEC for thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) on SOI.

If that platform matures into HVM (high-volume manufacturing), the valuation conversation changes completely.

You're not talking about a specialty analog/mixed-signal foundry anymore.

You're talking about a photonics-capable specialty foundry at the intersection of AI infrastructure, optical interconnects, and next-gen sensing.

That's a different multiple entirely.

My take:
Bernstein's upgrade is right on direction, wrong on magnitude.

The PT reflects the cyclical recovery. It doesn't reflect what XFAB becomes if the photonics bet hits.

Still bullish. Watching closely.

$XFAB

Bullish. $XFAb

text:
Aeluma $ALMU just made one of the most interesting hires I've seen from the company.

Brendan Moran spent 18 years at Lumileds helping take photonics products from R&D into high-volume manufacturing.
25+ products launched.

4+ billion units shipped.

Managed a mobile portfolio exceeding $300M in annual revenue.

This is not a scientist hire.
This is a commercialization hire.

Watch what companies DO, not what they SAY.

#Semiconductors #Photonics #SiliconPhotonics #ALMU

text:
Nvidia spent $6.5B on photonics in 8 weeks. Copper can't keep up with 100K-GPU clusters.

Feynman (2028) drops copper entirely - NVLink 8 runs on fiber. TSMC's CPO is in high-volume production. Besi: 10x power, 20x latency gains.

Here's the part nobody talks about: AI data centers need 36x more fiber than traditional racks. Cable lead times hit one year. Preform expansion takes 18-24 months.

The next AI bottleneck isn't chip supply. It's glass.

#SiliconPhotonics #Nvidia #AISupplyChain #CPO

text:
$SIVE AGM & Q1 2026 – The board upgrade that signals M&A readiness right now
 I’ve been reflecting on the AGM outcome and the Q1 transcript, and one thread stands out more clearly than before: this company is deliberately positioning itself for the next layer of acceleration.
 Vickram was direct about it:

“This brings a very experienced and balanced board composition for us, and in particular, tremendous executive credibility and experience with U.S. markets, as well as strong M&A experience as we look not only at organically growing our business, but also adjacencies, complementary portfolios as well for the future if that can help further accelerate growth for the company.”

That’s not just some corporate line. That’s the CEO openly saying they’re keeping the door open for smart acquisitions.

The new board members feel spot on for this phase. Joakim Nideborn as Vice Chairman with his listed-company CFO experience and Helena Svancar with her deep M&A background (including time as Head of M&A at $ERIC) these are exactly the kind of people you want around the table when you’re thinking about scaling faster than organic growth alone can take you.

I’ve seen this story play out with $SIVEF before. Back in 2017 they acquired CST Global, what became Sivers Photonics. It was a bold bet on III-V lasers and the Glasgow foundry. There was dilution, integration headaches, and it took several years before the payoff became obvious. But look where we are today: that same platform is right in the middle of the AI data center laser ramps, LiDAR production ($AEVA), and the $JBL pluggable collaboration. It completely transformed what $SIVE could do.

MixComm followed a similar path, added real wireless depth and U.S. muscle, and now it’s helping drive the SATCOM momentum with https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp / $YSS and the big IRIS² opportunity in Europe.
Both deals took time and patience, but they delivered real acceleration. I have a strong feeling we’re heading for something similar again. The setup just feels right: a nearly $800M pipeline that’s still growing fast, multiple 2027 ramps stacking up, and now a board with genuine M&A firepower. I’m actively digging into potential candidates right now, and when I’ve vetted something properly I’ll share my thoughts.
 What makes this moment particularly interesting is how the transcript frames the bigger picture. Vickram described being in the middle of three super cycles at once AI factories, space/SATCOM, and modern warfare and noted that $SIVE has differentiated technology that can meaningfully support elements of all three. He also highlighted the IRIS² European satellite infrastructure effort as a substantial long-term opportunity for the wireless business: a pipeline north of $80 million through 2030 (and that’s not even half the lifetime revenue potential), with $SIVE already engaged with five subcontractors in the final RFP stage for user terminals that start development in 2027. As an EU-based provider, they’re uniquely positioned on the sovereignty angle.
 The board changes, the explicit openness to adjacencies and M&A, the progress on U.S. dual-listing readiness, and the manufacturing capacity build-out all point in the same direction: $SIVE is building the foundation and the optionality to grow both organically and through smart additions at exactly the moment the secular tailwinds are strongest.
 I remain deeply convicted as a long-term shareholder. The transformation from development-led to product-led is progressing, the pipeline is a genuine lead indicator, and the company now has the governance and expertise in place to act on opportunities that can meaningfully accelerate the trajectory.
 Small volumes turning into bigger numbers and potentially even bigger ones, faster, with the right moves.

What are your thoughts? Did anything in the transcript or the board changes stand out to you? Or any areas you think would make sense for a potential acquisition? Would love to hear!

@FonteauO You clearly know this field much better than I do. 
Your point about wafer-scale, photonics and new cooling approaches makes sense. I agree cooling will probably change a lot. I’m just not sure heat as a constraint ever completely disappears and therfore instability. but ❤️
@FonteauO Using light (photonics) does not eliminate cooling requirements. But hopefully u r right

@ArtofSpecuycky Spot on. Optical interconnects (COHR, MRVL) and advanced packaging (AMKR) are the ultimate L0/L1 chokepoints. As GPU clusters scale, photonics isn't just a cycle; it's the physical bottleneck dictating L2 datacenter limits. Are you tracking glass substrates here?…

text:
My thesis was that stocks top with $SPCX.

Why?

Stock bros up 20x, crypto bros buying stock bros bags at ATH’s; the very same “retail” that was crushed at the top of every crypto bull cycle…

Everyone following stock KOL’s like @aleabitoreddit and ape whatever ticker is mentioned.

Trillion dollar IPO’s.

Literally the same sh** we’ve seen at the top of every crypto bull cycle.

Of course, the difference is these are real companies with real revenue, but timing does still matter unless you want to sit -30% under water for a couple years and HODL.

And at the end of the day, businesses fail, competition is fierce, there’s no way every single one of your bottleneck photonics glass substrate hydro-cooling picks are going to just keep going up over time.

Everyone’s a genius season- notice it when it’s there and start looking for the exit.

text:
$SIVE / $SIVEF Bull Case: Why Sivers Semiconductors could become the next $LITE

(Disclaimer-I have a position in Sivers)

Everyone wants the next GPU winner.

Few are looking at the companies enabling AI infrastructure behind the scenes.

That’s the Sivers thesis.

The opportunity isn’t becoming another Nvidia—it’s becoming part of the connectivity layer that AI depends on.

As AI clusters scale, compute is no longer the only bottleneck. Moving massive amounts of data efficiently is becoming just as important. More bandwidth, lower latency, lower power.

That’s where optical networking and photonics enter.

Sivers is positioning around:
• Optical engines
• External laser solutions
• Co-packaged optics (CPO)
• AI datacenter connectivity

The reason bulls compare it to $LITE:

Lumentum became valuable because optical components quietly became mission critical.

If AI infrastructure follows a similar path, the companies enabling data movement—not just compute—could see outsized value creation.

Sivers has also built relationships across the ecosystem through collaborations and development efforts with major manufacturing and photonics players. That doesn’t guarantee success—but it creates a path to relevance.

The bet here isn’t that Sivers dominates AI.

The bet is that it captures a small but important piece of a market growing exponentially.

Small company.
Big TAM.
Execution required.

That’s where asymmetric setups come from.

text:
If you're not buying today there's probably no reason to be checking your portfolio every five minutes
Close the app and go outside

It's always refreshing being around people in the real world who have absolutely no idea the market is down

I don't think this is the start of a bear market or some epic crash. If anything, the market seems to be getting a little ahead of itself on rate cut expectations

$MU reports tonight and I think it'll be one of the more important reads for the AI and photonics trade

Volatility is the price you pay for superior returns
It sucks but that's the game we play

text:
The following is an objective and comprehensive analysis of $NOK, $MRVL, and $MSFT, based on the latest publicly available financial reports and reliable sources (as of around June 24, 2026; the most recent quarter for NOK/MRVL/MSFT is their respective FY period; some Q2/Q3 updates are available).

1. $NOK (Nokia Corporation)

Company Overview: A global leader in telecommunications network infrastructure, with businesses covering mobile networks (5G/6G), optical networks, IP routing, and enterprise solutions. It is transforming into AI infrastructure (photonics, data center connectivity).

Main Revenue Sources: Network infrastructure; AI/cloud customer revenue has grown rapidly in recent years (increasing its share in the most recent quarter).

Competitive Advantages: Accumulated 5G/optical network technology, global operator relationships, and the acquisition of Infinera enhancing photonics capabilities; outstanding cost-effectiveness in the AI ​​data center interconnect field.

Revenue: Approximately $20 billion TTM, with AI-related growth of 49% in some quarters.

Profit Margin: Improved gross margin (approximately 45.5% recently), net margin approximately 4%.

FCF/Liabilities: Stable cash flow and manageable debt.

Valuation: Trailing P/E ~85x (relatively high), Forward P/E ~36x, P/S ~3.3x. Reasonable compared to historical cycles, but with a significant premium from AI transformation; attractive growth compared to telecom peers.

Industry/Sector Position: Telecom infrastructure sector, currently showing divergent performance driven by AI. The company has a solid position in 5G/photonics, and the sector trend is shifting towards AI data center interconnection.

Recent News and Catalysts (last 1-4 weeks):

Positive: Trump mentions NOK, expanded AI cooperation with Amazon/Google, growth in AI infrastructure orders.

Negative: Cyclical fluctuations in telecom spending, overall market correction pressure.

Impact: Short-term (1-3 months) significant earnings catalyst; long-term (6-12 months) accelerated growth if AI transformation is implemented effectively.

Risks and Opportunities:

Risks: Telecom capital expenditure cycle, intensified competition, execution risks.

Opportunities: AI photonics orders, 5G/6G deployment, successful transformation.

Overall Assessment: Valuation is reasonably high (forward valuation is reasonable, trailing reflects transformation expectations). Bull Case: AI infrastructure orders exceeded expectations, and profit margins continue to expand. Bear Case: Telecom spending slowed, and transformation fell short of expectations.

2. $MRVL (Marvell Technology)

Company Overview: A semiconductor company focusing on data centers, custom AI chips, electro-optical interconnects, and enterprise networks.

Main Revenue Sources: Data centers (AI-driven, significantly increasing to 75%+), custom silicon, and electro-optical products.

Competitive Advantages: Deep collaboration with NVIDIA, custom AI ASIC design capabilities, and leading data center interconnect technology.

Financial Health (FY2026 Data):

Revenue: Record $8.195 billion in FY2026 (YoY +42%); strong growth in recent quarters such as Q2 and FY2027.

Profit Margin: Non-GAAP gross margin ~59%, significantly improved.

FCF: Strong operating cash flow.

Valuation: P/S/P/E elevated due to high growth (depending on the latest price, market cap is in the tens of billions of dollars). Significantly improved compared to historical levels, competitive with AI semiconductor peers.

Industry/Sector Position: Semiconductor/AI Chip Sector, currently fluctuating. The company's position in the data center AI chip/interconnect field is rising; the sector trend is driven by AI capex but is experiencing a short-term pullback.

Recent News and Catalysts:

Positive: Strong AI demand, NVIDIA collaboration, upward revision of revenue guidance.

Negative: Overall selling pressure in the sector, high valuation correction.

Impact: Short-term earnings/order catalysts; long-term growth in custom AI silicon.

Risks and Opportunities:

Risks: Slowing AI spending, competition, valuation compression.

Opportunities: Custom AI chip orders, data center expansion.

Overall Assessment: Valuation reflects high growth, reasonably high (AI premium). Bull Case: Continued outperformance in AI orders, revenue acceleration. Bear Case: Slower cycle or increased competition leading to lower-than-expected growth.

3. $MSFT (Microsoft Corporation)

Company Overview: Technology giant, with businesses covering cloud (Azure), software (Office), AI (Copilot/OpenAI), games, etc.

Key Revenue Streams: Intelligent Cloud (dominated by Azure), Productivity & Business Processes.

Competitive Advantages: Cloud market leadership, AI integration (Copilot), enterprise ecosystem lock-in, OpenAI collaboration.

Financial Health (FY2026 Q3 data):

Revenue: Q3 $82.9 billion (YoY +18%), Cloud $54.5 billion (+29%).

text:
$POET 

(NASDAQ: POET)

One name that fits the "undiscovered/high-upside" profile in the current environment is POET Technologies. It develops advanced photonics (optical) technology, specifically its Optical Interposer platform, aimed at solving high-speed data connectivity bottlenecks in AI data centers and high-performance computing. 

Why it has high-upside potential:AI tailwinds: As AI models and GPU clusters demand faster, more efficient interconnects, POET's technology aims to integrate optics and electronics on a single chip—smaller, cheaper, faster, and lower power than traditional solutions.
Growth trajectory: Transitioning from development/pre-revenue to commercialization. Analysts project significant revenue ramp in 2026–2027 if partnerships and orders scale (e.g., initial orders and ecosystem building noted in recent updates).
Valuation upside: Trading at levels that reflect execution risk, but optimistic scenarios (commercialization success) point to substantial re-rating potential. Analyst targets (where available) and models have shown meaningful upside from recent prices.
Niche/lower visibility: It's not a household name like big AI plays (NVDA, etc.), fitting the "undiscovered" description for many investors.

Major risks (these are substantial):Pre-commercial or early-stage revenue — execution, manufacturing scale-up, and customer adoption are uncertain.
High volatility and potential dilution (common in growth tech).
Competition and technology risk — photonics is complex; delays or failures could hurt the stock severely.
Cash burn and need for funding.
