text:
$SVCO

+17% this past week.

Don’t miss the next $SNPS 

Bookmark. Repost. SUB $1 to support.
Also $CELH 😉

Only had one try let’s get it x

If I was doing what I am now, following these breakouts earlier this year, you could have bought this around $6.

I promise I’ll keep finding them x fam. 

At just a ~$435M market cap, $SVCO remains one of the smallest publicly traded companies providing critical software infrastructure to the semiconductor industry.

When investors talk about $ARM, $SNPS, $CDNS, $TSM, $MU, $ON, $GFS, and $XFAB, they should also be paying attention to $SVCO.

Silvaco is a true semiconductor “picks and shovels” company.

While most investors focus on who manufactures chips, Silvaco provides the software used to design, simulate, optimize, and validate chips before a single wafer is produced.

Building and testing wafers is expensive, time-consuming, and can cost millions per iteration. Silvaco’s Victory TCAD platform allows engineers to create digital twins of manufacturing processes and device architectures, testing thousands of scenarios virtually before committing to silicon.

Its software models deposition, etching, ion implantation, oxidation, thermal behavior, reliability, quantum effects, and complete 2D/3D process flows.

These tools are critical for:

• AI chips and HBM memory.
• SiC and GaN power semiconductors.
• Photonics and optical interconnects.
• Automotive semiconductors.
• Displays, micro-LEDs, and advanced packaging.

Without accurate TCAD simulation, fabs cannot efficiently develop next-generation manufacturing processes, improve yields, or reduce development costs.

This is why Silvaco has become deeply embedded throughout the semiconductor ecosystem.

The company serves 800+ customers and 200+ universities across foundries, IDMs, fabless designers, equipment makers, and research institutions.

Key ecosystem relationships include:

• Strategic investment and expanded partnership from $MU.
• Technology relationships with $TSM, $UMC, $GFS, $ON, and $XFAB.
• Participation in TSMC’s Open Innovation Platform.
• Expanded SiC partnership with APEC.
• MCU development partnership with ITRI.
• GaN collaboration with Fraunhofer ISIT.

The company’s biggest growth driver is FTCO™ (Fab Technology Co-Optimization).

FTCO combines TCAD simulations, manufacturing data, machine learning, and AI-driven optimization to create digital twins of semiconductor production.

As semiconductor complexity increases, AI-driven digital twins become increasingly valuable.

Momentum continues building:

• Q1 2026 revenue grew 26% YoY to $17.8M.
• Total bookings increased 26% YoY to $17.2M.
• TCAD bookings increased ~50% YoY.
• TCAD bookings surged 70% QoQ in Q4 2025.
• SIP revenue exploded ~270% YoY.
• Backlog reached ~$46.6M.
• Gross margins exceeded 86%.
• Double-digit growth expected throughout 2026.
• Non-GAAP profitability targeted in 2026.

The Semiconductor IP business is also ramping rapidly.

Following the Mixel acquisition, Silvaco launched the industry’s first MIPI C-PHY/D-PHY Combo IP on TSMC N2P and released 160+ new IP products in Q1 alone, including 80+ MIPI PHY solutions targeting mobile, automotive, IoT, wearables, and AR/VR.

Additional acquisitions including Mixel, Tech-X, and portions of Cadence’s OPC business expanded Silvaco’s addressable market by roughly $600M while strengthening its position across photonics, plasma simulation, semiconductor IP, and advanced process development.

What makes the story interesting is the size.

$SVCO ≈ $435M market cap.

$SNPS ≈ $80B+ market cap.

$CDNS ≈ $100B+ market cap.

$ARM ≈ $450B+ market cap.

The company is one of the few pure-play ways to invest in TCAD, process simulation, AI-powered digital twins, power semiconductors, photonics, and semiconductor IP all in one company.

Semiconductor picks and shovels.

86%+ gross margins.

$46.6M backlog.

800+ customers.

Critical technology behind the chip.

Keep $SVCO on your radar.

When investors talk about $ARM, $SNPS, $CDNS, $TSM, $MU, $ON, $GFS, and $XFAB, they should also be paying attention to $SVCO. Silvaco is a true semiconductor “picks and shovels” company. While most investors focus on who manufactures chips, Silvaco provides the software used to design, simulate, optimize, and validate chips before a single wafer is produced. Building and testing wafers is expensive, time-consuming, and can cost millions per iteration. Silvaco’s Victory TCAD platform allows engineers to create digital twins of manufacturing processes and device architectures, testing thousands of scenarios virtually before committing to silicon. Its software models deposition, etching, ion implantation, oxidation, thermal behavior, reliability, quantum effects, and complete 2D/3D process flows. These tools are critical for: • AI chips and HBM memory. • SiC and GaN power semiconductors. • Photonics and optical interconnects. • Automotive semiconductors. • Displays, micro-LEDs, and advanced packaging. Without accurate TCAD simulation, fabs cannot efficiently develop next-generation manufacturing processes, improve yields, or reduce development costs. This is why Silvaco has become deeply embedded throughout the semiconductor ecosystem. The company serves 800+ customers and 200+ universities across foundries, IDMs, fabless designers, equipment makers, and research institutions. Key ecosystem relationships include: • Strategic investment and expanded partnership from $MU. • Technology relationships with $TSM, $UMC, $GFS, $ON, and $XFAB. • Participation in TSMC’s Open Innovation Platform. • Expanded SiC partnership with APEC. • MCU development partnership with ITRI. • GaN collaboration with Fraunhofer ISIT. The company’s biggest growth driver is FTCO™ (Fab Technology Co-Optimization). FTCO combines TCAD simulations, manufacturing data, machine learning, and AI-driven optimization to create digital twins of semiconductor production. As semiconductor complexity increases, AI-driven digital twins become increasingly valuable. The Semiconductor IP business is also ramping rapidly. Additional acquisitions including Mixel, Tech-X, and portions of Cadence’s OPC business expanded Silvaco’s addressable market by roughly $600M while strengthening its position across photonics, plasma simulation, semiconductor IP, and advanced process development. What makes the story interesting is the size. $SVCO ≈ $435M market cap. $SNPS ≈ $80B+ market cap. $CDNS ≈ $100B+ market cap. $ARM ≈ $450B+ market cap. text:
Clean fib levels update, The names I’m most convicted on from this list:
• $MRVL – AI connectivity & custom silicon tailwinds
• $PLTR – enterprise AI 
• $MU – HBM memory ramp
• $IREN – power moat + hyperscaler pipeline (MSFT/NVDA exposure)
• $LITE – photonics/opticals for 800G/1.6T+ interconnects
• $IONQ – quantum timeline accelerating
• $RKLB – space infrastructure buildout

Would’ve loved to see $TE but overall great list. Appreciate the work.

text:
⚡ TECH, EV & GADGETS ROUNDUP — June 20, 2026

1️⃣ IONQ WHITE PAPER: QUANTUM COMPUTING FUTURE LOOKS LIKE EARLY INTERNET

IonQ co-founder Chris Monroe published a new white paper outlining a vision for quantum computing that mirrors how the early internet evolved: rather than relying on one massive machine, the future belongs to smaller, high-quality quantum systems connected by light. This distributed approach — already validated by recent physics breakthroughs — could dramatically accelerate the path to practical quantum computing. The paper, posted on arXiv, represents a significant shift in how leading quantum researchers envision scaling the technology.
@IonQ_Inc

2️⃣ AI INFRASTRUCTURE ROADMAP THROUGH 2030: FROM SEMICONDUCTORS TO QUANTUM

A comprehensive analysis maps the investment and technology roadmap for AI infrastructure over the next four years. The thesis traces a clear progression: semiconductors and memory form the foundation through 2027, followed by photonics and optical networking as AI clusters scale. Beyond that, the cycle moves into energy storage and renewables (driven by data center power demands), physical AI and robotics by 2027-2028, space infrastructure, nuclear energy by 2028-2030, and finally quantum computing later in the decade. The underlying principle: AI infrastructure first, applications second, power generation third.
@InvestmentGuru_

3️⃣ QUANTUMSCAPE PARTNERS WITH HONDA ON SOLID-STATE BATTERY RESEARCH

QuantumScape announced a multi-year joint research agreement with Honda R&D, marking another major automaker validating the company's solid-state battery technology. This partnership adds to growing industry confidence in solid-state batteries as the next generation of EV powertrains, promising higher energy density, faster charging, and improved safety over conventional lithium-ion cells. The collaboration signals that solid-state batteries are moving closer to commercial production timelines.
@donaldjdean

4️⃣ FEDERAL EV CHARGING GRANTS UNFROZEN AND MOVING TO LOCAL LEVEL

Federal EV charging grants have been officially unfrozen and are now being distributed to local municipalities. Property owners and businesses can access funding for installing charging infrastructure, following a period where these grants were paused. This development could accelerate the buildout of public and private EV charging networks at the community level, addressing one of the key barriers to electric vehicle adoption.
@StayNChargeApp

5️⃣ AMAZON AWS CHALLENGES NVIDIA BY SELLING TRAINIUM CHIPS TO OTHER DATA CENTERS

Amazon is expanding beyond its own infrastructure needs by selling its custom Trainium AI chips to external data centers, directly challenging NVIDIA's dominance in the AI accelerator market. AWS CEO Andy Jassy described this as a massive opportunity, potentially opening a $50 billion competition in custom silicon for AI workloads. This move reflects a broader trend of hyperscalers leveraging their in-house chip designs to compete with traditional semiconductor giants.
@GadgetsTimesRD

6️⃣ GOLDMAN SACHS CUTS GLOBAL SMARTPHONE MARKET ESTIMATES ON MEMORY COSTS

Goldman Sachs has trimmed its global smartphone market forecasts, citing elevated memory component costs as the primary headwind. Rising prices for DRAM and other memory chips are squeezing profit margins across the smartphone industry, potentially leading to higher consumer prices or slower shipment volumes. This highlights the interconnectedness of the semiconductor supply chain and its ripple effects on consumer electronics markets.
@Michael34952

7️⃣ MULTIPLE COMPANIES BUILDING DATA CENTERS IN LOW EARTH ORBIT

Several companies are developing data center infrastructure in Low Earth Orbit, including Starcloud, Axiom Space, OrbitsEdge, LEOcloud, Qunex Tech Corp, and Sophia Space. This emerging sector aims to leverage space-based computing for edge processing, satellite communications, and reduced-latency applications. As ground-based data centers face power and space constraints, orbital computing could become a complementary layer for specific workloads, though the technology remains in early development stages.
@Luodetlahti

💭 The tech landscape continues to accelerate across multiple frontiers simultaneously. Quantum computing researchers are rethinking architecture with distributed networks, solid-state batteries gain automaker validation, and hyperscalers are breaking NVIDIA's GPU monopoly with custom silicon. Meanwhile, the physical infrastructure supporting all this AI compute — from memory chips to orbital data centers — is becoming the defining battleground. The companies winning this cycle may not be building AI models at all, but rather the pipes, power, and processors that make AI possible.

Which emerging technology do you think will have the biggest real-world impact by 2030? 👇

#TechNews #QuantumComputing #SolidStateBatteries #AIInfrastructure #EVCharging #Semiconductors #SpaceComputing

This development could accelerate the buildout of public and private EV charging networks at the community level, addressing one of the key barriers to electric vehicle adoption. @StayNChargeApp 5️⃣ AMAZON AWS CHALLENGES NVIDIA BY SELLING TRAINIUM CHIPS TO OTHER DATA CENTERS Amazon is expanding beyond its own infrastructure needs by selling its custom Trainium AI chips to external data centers, directly challenging NVIDIA's dominance in the AI accelerator market. AWS CEO Andy Jassy described this as a massive opportunity, potentially opening a $50 billion competition in custom silicon for AI workloads. This move reflects a broader trend of hyperscalers leveraging their in-house chip designs to compete with traditional semiconductor giants. @GadgetsTimesRD 6️⃣ GOLDMAN SACHS CUTS GLOBAL SMARTPHONE MARKET ESTIMATES ON MEMORY COSTS Goldman Sachs has trimmed its global smartphone market forecasts, citing elevated memory component costs as the primary headwind. Rising prices for DRAM and other memory chips are squeezing profit margins across the smartphone industry, potentially leading to higher consumer prices or slower shipment volumes. This highlights the interconnectedness of the semiconductor supply chain and its ripple effects on consumer electronics markets. @Michael34952 7️⃣ MULTIPLE COMPANIES BUILDING DATA CENTERS IN LOW EARTH ORBIT Several companies are developing data center infrastructure in Low Earth Orbit, including Starcloud, Axiom Space, OrbitsEdge, LEOcloud, Qunex Tech Corp, and Sophia Space. This emerging sector aims to leverage space-based computing for edge processing, satellite communications, and reduced-latency applications. As ground-based data centers face power and space constraints, orbital computing could become a complementary layer for specific workloads, though the technology remains in early development stages. @Luodetlahti 💭 The tech landscape continues to accelerate across multiple frontiers simultaneously. Quantum computing researchers are rethinking architecture with distributed networks, solid-state batteries gain automaker validation, and hyperscalers are breaking NVIDIA's GPU monopoly with custom silicon. Meanwhile, the physical infrastructure supporting all this AI compute — from memory chips to orbital data centers — is becoming the defining battleground. The companies winning this cycle may not be building AI models at all, but rather the pipes, power, and processors that make AI possible.
